name: Microsoft PlayFab
what_it_does: Microsoft Azure-backed game backend BaaS that provides player authentication, leaderboards, inventory, matchmaking, economy, and live operations for games across all platforms.
use_cases: Automate player segment creation and targeted push notification campaigns based on in-game behavior
Sync player inventory and virtual currency grants in response to purchase webhooks from payment processors
Generate and distribute daily/weekly leaderboard reward payouts to top-ranked players via CloudScript
Create and update game economy catalog items (prices, bundles, sale events) programmatically for live ops
Monitor active player counts and matchmaking queue depth to auto-scale game server capacity
not_for: Non-game applications — PlayFab features (economy, matchmaking, CloudScript) are game-domain-specific
Real-time physics or authoritative game simulation — PlayFab handles meta-game services, not gameplay itself
Replacing a full database — player data storage has schema constraints not suited for arbitrary relational data
best_when: Building a live-service game that needs player auth, leaderboards, economy, and analytics without managing backend infrastructure.
avoid_when: Your game requires ultra-low-latency authoritative server logic or you need full control over the data schema and infrastructure.

auth.notes: Title Secret Key is used for server-to-server calls (admin/server APIs). Client APIs use per-player session tokens obtained after login. Entity tokens are the newer unified auth model. Secret key must never be embedded in client builds.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free up to 100,000 monthly active users (MAU) with full feature access including leaderboards, economy, and CloudScript.
pricing.paid_tiers: Essentials at $299/month (up to 1M MAU), higher tiers by custom quote. Overage charges apply per MAU beyond tier.
pricing.notes: Free tier is genuinely generous for indie games. Azure integration and advanced add-ons (Insights, Multiplayer Servers) billed separately via Azure.

performance.rate_limits: Rate limits vary by API category; admin APIs default to 32 requests/second per title; player-facing APIs scale with MAU tier. CloudScript has execution time limits (10s default).

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Errors return a numeric error code plus a human-readable 'errorMessage' field. The error code catalog is well-documented but agents must map numeric codes to meanings; not all errors include remediation hints.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Write operations (grant items, update statistics) are generally not idempotent — duplicate calls can double-grant items. Use CloudScript transactions or check-then-act patterns for safety.
known_agent_gotchas: Title Secret Key must be kept server-side; if an agent runs in a client context it must proxy through a server or CloudScript
Player data keys are case-sensitive and silently create new keys on typos rather than returning an error
CloudScript function names are case-sensitive and calling a nonexistent function returns a generic error not a 404
Leaderboard statistics must be pre-declared in the title settings before they can be written via API
Entity token expiry (24h default) requires automatic re-authentication; agents that cache tokens will fail silently after expiry

security_score_components.security_notes: Title Secret Key is a single high-privilege credential with no scoping — compromise grants full admin access to the title. Entity model adds some granularity but server-side key management is critical. Azure infrastructure provides strong underlying security.
